description: 15 shared practice questions applicable to all archetypes: 5 behavioral (STAR format), 5 estimation/market sizing, 5 MCQ concept checks

questions.text: Tell me about a time when you had to make a product decision with incomplete data. How did you approach it, and what was the outcome?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Strong answers follow STAR format clearly. Situation: specific context with constraints (timeline, missing data type). Task: what decision needed to be made and why it couldn't wait. Action: how they gathered available signals, used proxies, set up reversibility, or de-risked the decision. Result: outcome with specific metrics if possible, plus learnings applied later. Look for: comfort with ambiguity, structured decision-making process, awareness of risks taken.

strong_signals: Clear proxy metrics or signals used
Reflected on what they'd do differently

questions.text: Describe a situation where you disagreed with a stakeholder (engineer, designer, manager, or executive) about a product direction. How did you handle it?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Situation: who disagreed, what was at stake, why opinions differed. Task: candidate's role and what resolution was needed. Action: how they sought to understand the other perspective, what data or frameworks they used to find common ground, how they communicated their position. Result: resolution reached, relationship preserved, and learning about collaboration. Red flag: candidates who 'won' by hierarchy or politics without genuine alignment.

weak_signals: No reflection on own blind spots
strong_signals: Showed genuine curiosity about opposing view
Used data or customer evidence to ground discussion
Found compromise or acknowledged when they were wrong
Maintained relationship while advocating for position

questions.text: Tell me about a product or feature you shipped that didn't perform as expected. What happened, and what did you learn?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Situation: what was shipped, what metrics were expected, what actually happened. Task: candidate's responsibility and accountability. Action: how they diagnosed the gap (data analysis, user research, technical investigation), what corrective actions were taken. Result: whether it was fixed, deprecated, or iterated — and specific learnings applied to future work. Strong answers show ownership without excessive self-flagellation.

strong_signals: Owned the outcome regardless of role
Concrete changes to process or approach going forward

questions.text: Describe a time when you had to influence without authority — getting something done when you didn't have direct control over the people or resources involved.
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Situation: cross-functional context where candidate lacked formal authority. Task: what outcome they needed and who they needed to influence. Action: how they built alignment (understanding others' incentives, finding shared goals, creating urgency, demonstrating value). Result: did they achieve the goal? How did the relationship evolve? Strong answers show strategic thinking about stakeholder motivations.

strong_signals: Built trust before asking for help
Showed patience and long-term relationship building

questions.text: Tell me about a time when you had to rapidly learn a new domain or technology to make a product decision. How did you get up to speed?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Situation: unfamiliar domain with time pressure. Task: what decision hinged on this learning. Action: specific learning tactics — talking to experts, reading documentation, building prototypes, asking structured questions. Result: quality of decision made and speed of ramp-up. Look for: intellectual curiosity, humility, structured approach to learning.

strong_signals: Asked clarifying questions to validate understanding
Made sound decision despite limited time

questions.text: How many auto rickshaws are there in Bangalore?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Strong approach: 1) Frame the problem — define what counts as an auto rickshaw (registered, operational). 2) Choose an estimation method — supply-side (drivers, vehicles per driver) or demand-side (trips per day, autos needed to serve demand). 3) Build from anchors — Bangalore population (~12M), percentage who use autos, trips per user per day, trips per auto per day. 4) Sanity check — compare to known data points (if any). Reasonable range: 100,000-200,000 autos. The number matters less than the structure.

weak_signals: Used only one approach without sanity check
strong_signals: Sanity checked against intuition or known data

questions.text: Estimate the annual revenue of Zomato's food delivery business in India.
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Approach 1 (demand-side): Active users × orders per user per month × average order value × take rate × 12. Approach 2 (supply-side): Number of restaurant partners × orders per restaurant per day × average order value × take rate × 365. Key assumptions: 50M+ monthly active users, 3-4 orders per user per month, ₹350-400 AOV, 20-25% take rate. Should arrive at ₹8,000-15,000 crore range. Bonus: acknowledge that 'revenue' could mean GMV vs. net revenue (after restaurant payouts).

weak_signals: Unrealistic assumptions (e.g., 10 orders per user per week)
No awareness of take rate concept
strong_signals: Cross-checked with public data if mentioned

questions.text: How would you estimate the number of smartphones sold in India last year?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Approach 1 (stock-based): Total smartphone users in India × replacement rate. ~700M smartphone users, 2.5-3 year replacement cycle = 230-280M per year. Approach 2 (flow-based): New smartphone buyers + replacement buyers. New buyers shrinking as penetration saturates. Should get to 150-200M range (actual is ~150M). Key: distinguish 'sold' from 'shipped' and account for feature phone to smartphone migration slowing.

weak_signals: Used total population instead of smartphone users
strong_signals: Distinguished smartphone users from total population
Considered both new and replacement demand

questions.text: Estimate the market size for premium coffee (₹200+ per cup) in Indian metros.
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Define scope: metros (Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore, Chennai, Hyderabad, Pune, Kolkata). Define premium: ₹200+ (Starbucks, Blue Tokai, third wave). Approach: Metro population (~80M) → income bracket that can afford (top 10-15%: ~10M) → % who drink premium coffee (30-40%: ~3-4M) → frequency (1-2x per week: 6 cups/month) → average spend (₹250). Monthly: 3.5M × 6 × 250 = ₹5,250 crore/year. Sanity check against Starbucks India revenue + competitors.

strong_signals: Sanity check against known player data

questions.text: How many weddings happen in India each year, and what's the total market size for wedding-related services?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Part 1 - Number of weddings: India population (~1.4B) × marriage rate. Alternatively: marriageable population (ages 20-35, ~300M) ÷ average marriage age span × marriage rate (~90%). Should get to 8-12M weddings per year. Part 2 - Market size: Segment by income (budget/mid/luxury). Average wedding spend by segment. Weight by distribution. Budget (₹5-10L) × 60%, Mid (₹10-30L) × 30%, Luxury (₹30L+) × 10%. Total: ₹4-5 lakh crore market. Break down by service: venue, catering, photography, jewelry, clothing, etc.

weak_signals: Used total population without age filtering
Single average wedding cost for all segments
Forgot that each wedding involves 2 families
strong_signals: Income segmentation with different spend levels
Cross-checked against industry reports if mentioned

questions.text: In the STAR interview format, what does the 'A' stand for, and why is it the most important part?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: A stands for Action. It's the most important because interviewers want to understand YOUR specific contributions, decisions, and behaviors — not just what happened or the outcome.

strong_signals: Understands Action is about personal contribution

mcq_options.text: Assessment — evaluating the situation is the foundation for good decisions
mcq_options.explanation: A stands for Action, not Assessment. While evaluation matters, STAR focuses on what you did.

mcq_options.text: Action — it shows YOUR specific contributions and decisions, not just what happened
mcq_options.explanation: Correct. The Action section demonstrates your personal impact, skills, and decision-making approach.

mcq_options.text: Achievement — results matter most in any behavioral question
mcq_options.explanation: A stands for Action. Results are covered in R (Result), which is important but Action shows how you operate.

mcq_options.text: Analysis — breaking down the problem is the key PM skill
mcq_options.explanation: A stands for Action. Analysis might be part of your action, but STAR specifically asks about what you did.

questions.text: When estimating market size, what's the key difference between TAM, SAM, and SOM?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: TAM = Total Addressable Market (everyone who could theoretically buy). SAM = Serviceable Addressable Market (the portion you could reach with your current model). SOM = Serviceable Obtainable Market (what you can realistically capture).

strong_signals: Clear understanding of the funnel from theoretical to realistic

mcq_options.text: TAM is current market, SAM is growth potential, SOM is competition's share
mcq_options.explanation: This mischaracterizes all three. TAM is total theoretical market, not current market.

mcq_options.text: TAM is total theoretical market, SAM is what you could serve, SOM is what you can realistically capture
mcq_options.explanation: Correct. TAM → SAM → SOM represents a funnel from theoretical maximum to realistic target.

mcq_options.text: They're different ways of measuring the same market; use whichever sounds best
mcq_options.explanation: They represent different scopes. Using the wrong one misleads investors and planning.

mcq_options.text: TAM is for startups, SAM is for growth companies, SOM is for mature companies
mcq_options.explanation: All three are relevant for any company. They represent scope of market, not company stage.

questions.text: A user says 'I want the app to load faster.' What's the best PM response?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: The best response digs into the underlying need. Ask follow-up questions to understand context, use case, and what 'faster' means to them.

weak_signals: Takes request at face value without investigation
strong_signals: Seeks to understand the underlying job-to-be-done

mcq_options.text: Add it to the backlog and prioritize based on how many users request it
mcq_options.explanation: This treats the symptom without understanding the cause. Vote-counting isn't product strategy.

mcq_options.text: Tell engineering to optimize performance across the board
mcq_options.explanation: This is too vague and doesn't address the specific user need or context.

mcq_options.text: Ask follow-up questions: 'When does it feel slow? What are you trying to do when it's slow?'
mcq_options.explanation: Correct. Understanding context reveals the real problem — maybe it's slow on mobile data, or during checkout, or only at certain times.

mcq_options.text: Check analytics to see actual load times and decide if the complaint is valid
mcq_options.explanation: Data is useful but doesn't replace understanding user context. Perception matters even if metrics look fine.

questions.text: In a Fermi estimation, why is it important to state your assumptions explicitly?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: Explicit assumptions let the interviewer evaluate your reasoning, make the estimate debuggable, and allow for adjustment if an assumption is corrected.

mcq_options.text: So the interviewer can correct wrong assumptions and give you the right answer
mcq_options.explanation: Interviewers rarely give you 'right' answers. The point is evaluating your reasoning, not getting to a specific number.

mcq_options.text: To show off how much you know about the industry
mcq_options.explanation: Estimations test reasoning, not industry knowledge. Reasonable assumptions based on logic are better than memorized facts.

mcq_options.text: So your reasoning is transparent, debuggable, and can be adjusted if an assumption is off
mcq_options.explanation: Correct. Explicit assumptions show your thought process, let others verify your logic, and make the estimate adjustable.

mcq_options.text: Because you'll be graded on the accuracy of your final number
mcq_options.explanation: Estimation interviews grade reasoning, not accuracy. A well-reasoned estimate that's off by 2x beats a lucky guess.

questions.text: When answering 'Tell me about a time you failed,' what's the most important element to include?
questions.ideal_answer_outline: The most important element is what you learned and how you applied it. This shows growth mindset and self-awareness.

weak_signals: Focuses only on the failure itself or blaming others

mcq_options.text: A detailed explanation of why the failure wasn't really your fault
mcq_options.explanation: Deflecting blame is a red flag. Interviewers want to see ownership and self-awareness.

mcq_options.text: The specific metrics that showed the failure (to prove it was real)
mcq_options.explanation: Metrics help, but the learning matters more than proving the failure was significant.

mcq_options.text: What you learned and how you applied that learning to future situations
mcq_options.explanation: Correct. This demonstrates growth mindset, self-awareness, and the ability to improve — key PM traits.

mcq_options.text: How you eventually turned the failure into a success
mcq_options.explanation: Not every failure gets 'fixed.' The question tests how you handle and learn from failure, not just whether you recovered.

metadata.author: Rudra
metadata.notes: STAR format expected for behavioral questions. Estimation questions test structured approach. MCQs are quick concept checks (30-60 seconds).
